one_line_takeaway: An autonomous LLM agent starting from just a name and affiliation can reconstruct human-verified personal profiles (up to ~100 facts, >90% fact accuracy, ~$3 and minutes of compute) that exceed human-analyst coverage and speed, while none of the tested LLM-vendor, victim, or defense-side mitigations meaningfully block the attack.
key_finding: On the 30-scholar dataset (Table 1), search-less LLMs are largely infeasible (e.g. DeepSeek V3 averages only 2.90 URLs/facts with 0% accuracy reported for that row). Adding search drops refusal rates near 0% for most models but coverage stays modest (e.g. GPT-4o w/ search: 3.90 URLs, 7.60 facts, 77.78% URL acc, 70.61% fact acc). IcebergExplorer substantially outperforms all baselines and human experts: powered by GPT-4.1 it discovers 100.70 facts and 13.93 URLs per target with 99.54% URL accuracy and 79.21% fact accuracy at a 3.33% refusal rate; powered by Gemini 2.5 Pro it reaches 95.93 facts with 93.05% fact accuracy and an 83.33% valid-photo rate (all other baselines scored 0.00% on photo identification). Human experts averaged only 15.40 facts in 1094.46s (~18 min), versus IcebergExplorer/GPT-4o finding 4.5x more facts in 315.04s (~5 min); Gemini 2.5 Pro cost under $3 per target at $5/M output and $0.626/M input token pricing. In the separate real-world 30-participant consented study (Table 3, GPT-4.1 backbone), the agent achieved 0% refusal, 8.73 URLs and 67.83 facts per person in 361.46s, with 92.33% participant-confirmed fact accuracy, 70.43% URL accuracy, and 60% photo accuracy. Metadata analysis of the agent's own collected assets found 15 of 49 PDFs exposed author/producer metadata and 7 of 49 images retained EXIF data (GPS/device/timestamp). Defense tests found existing mitigations essentially ineffective: an LLM system-prompt guardrail produced 0.00% refusal at nearly every attack stage across Qwen3 Coder/Gemini 2.5 Pro/GPT-4o/GPT-4.1 (Table 5, one exception: 6.88% for GPT-4o at the reasoning stage), and victim-side prompt-injection/context-ignoring defenses (adapted from prior work) achieved close to 0% blocking success across all models (Table 6, best case 0.23% for Gemini 2.5 Pro). A downstream-harm test showed profiles mined by IcebergExplorer produced synthetic recommendation content (job/product/title) that won 70-96.67% of blind pairwise preference comparisons against baselines (Table 4).
core_contribution: The paper introduces PrivacyIceberg, a three-tier taxonomy of human-centric privacy risk from LLM-driven profiling (DII: Direct Identifiable Information retrievable by keyword search; CII: Contextually Inferred Information derived via LLM reasoning over a single source; AMI: Aggregated Mosaic Information synthesized by an agent correlating multiple DII/CII across unrelated sources), and IcebergExplorer, an autonomous multi-stage agent (iterative query refinement, LLM-as-judge snippet filtering, content-reduction extraction, and Add/Update/Delete knowledge-base maintenance with cross-source 'mosaic insight' synthesis) that emulates a stranger adversary to reconstruct personal profiles starting only from a name and affiliation. The authors validate the taxonomy with a 60-participant user study, benchmark IcebergExplorer against plain LLMs, LLMs+search, LLMs+search&thinking, and Deep Research agents, run a 30-scholar simulated-consent evaluation plus a separate 30-participant real-world consented case study, test three candidate defenses (LLM system-prompt guardrails, victim-side prompt-injection/context-ignoring defenses, and publisher-side structural mitigations), and derive six root causes of exposure (persistence, linkability, readability, identifiability, inferability, composability).
threat_model: Grounded in Contextual Integrity theory. Three roles: the Victim (subject), the Information Publisher (sender, e.g. universities/employers who post benign content), and a Stranger Adversary (recipient) who starts with minimal seed information (full name + primary affiliation, obtainable from venues like OpenReview/Google Scholar). The adversary uses only publicly accessible tools -- search engines and LLM/agent systems with web-search/crawling capability -- to maximize three success criteria: quantity of unique facts, factual accuracy, and sensitivity of aggregated information. Motivations enumerated: malicious doxing/phishing/social engineering, commercial headhunting/candidate screening, and platform-driven profile enrichment for personalization. The violation is framed as a breach of the 'limited-purpose' transmission principle (data published for one purpose repurposed for profiling), not unauthorized access -- all data collection in the paper's own experiments was restricted to publicly accessible pages, respected robots.txt and platform ToS, and was IRB-approved (ID 202432).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: manual fact verification (~8,000 facts for one tool) was labor-intensive and capped the dataset at 30 individuals, limiting statistical generalizability; the primary study population skewed toward scholars with unusually high public information exposure, so the measured leakage likely represents an upper bound rather than a population-representative estimate; findings are a snapshot of a rapidly evolving capability/privacy-norm landscape and may not transfer across cultures or future model generations. The paper also notes the tested defenses (system-prompt guardrails, prompt-injection-as-defense) were 'largely symbolic and ineffective' against the multi-stage attack, meaning no effective mitigation is demonstrated -- only diagnosed as needed.

datasets_used: 30 first authors of a recent CS conference (simulated/consented profiling benchmark with manual ground-truth verification, ~8,000 facts verified)
60-participant user study for Perceived Privacy Invasion Severity (PPIS) scoring of DII/CII/AMI scenarios
30 voluntary real-world participants (diverse backgrounds/occupations/ages/genders) for the consented real-world case study
10 major social media/AI-platform privacy policies reviewed (Appendix D)
One social-media case study (13,000+ likes, 2,159 comments) used for qualitative thematic coding of public reaction
quantitative_results: IcebergExplorer (GPT-4.1): 100.70 facts, 13.93 URLs, 99.54% URL accuracy, 79.21% fact accuracy, 76.67% photo accuracy, 3.33% refusal rate, 810.38s per target
IcebergExplorer (Gemini 2.5 Pro): 95.93 facts, 12.80 URLs, 100.00% URL accuracy, 93.05% fact accuracy, 83.33% photo accuracy, 3.33% refusal rate
IcebergExplorer (GPT-4o): 70.32 facts, 11.86 URLs, 100.00% URL accuracy, 92.44% fact accuracy, 77.27% photo accuracy, 30.00% refusal rate, 315.04s per target
Human experts: 15.40 facts, 14.60 URLs, 100.00% URL/fact accuracy, 40.00% photo accuracy, 1094.46s per target
Best non-IcebergExplorer baseline (LLM w/ Search&Thinking, GPT-4o): 9.20 facts, 6.00 URLs, 91.67% URL accuracy, 92.39% fact accuracy
Real-world consented study (IcebergExplorer/GPT-4.1): 67.83 facts, 8.73 URLs, 70.43% URL accuracy, 92.33% fact accuracy, 60.00% photo accuracy, 0.00% refusal, 361.46s per target
Attack cost under $3 per target (Gemini 2.5 Pro, at $5/M output + $0.626/M input tokens)
System-prompt guardrail defense: ~0.00% refusal across attack stages for all 4 backbone models except 6.88% (GPT-4o, reasoning stage)
Victim-side prompt-injection/context-ignoring defense: ~0.00% blocking success rate, best case 0.23% (Gemini 2.5 Pro)
Downstream harm win rates: IcebergExplorer-derived profiles won 70.00-96.67% of blind pairwise preference tests for job/product/title recommendation content vs. baseline-derived profiles
Metadata leakage in agent-collected assets: 15/49 PDFs exposed author/producer metadata (only 2/49 encrypted); 7/49 images retained EXIF data
50% of surveyed platforms explicitly disclosed reusing user-published data for LLM inference
baselines_compared: LLMs without search (DeepSeek V3, Doubao 1.5 Pro, Qwen3 Coder, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o)
LLMs with search (DeepSeek V3, Doubao 1.5 Pro, Qwen3 Coder, GPT-4o, Perplexity)
LLMs with search & thinking (DeepSeek R1, Doubao 1.5 Pro, Qwen3 Coder, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o)
Deep Research agents (Meta AI, Perplexity, Qwen3, Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o)
No-defense condition vs. system-prompt guardrail defense and context-ignoring/injected-data (CI/ID) victim-side defenses
